Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place. I tried to set aside time each day, but with my anxiety being what it is, some days I just couldn't focus at all. I'd usually listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, since that's how I learn best. I aimed for about 15 hours a week, but the quality of those hours varied a lot depending on how I was feeling emotionally.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, honestly. I wanted to be consistent, but my anxiety would sometimes paralyze me for days, and then I'd have to cram everything near deadlines. When I was feeling okay, I'd try to study in the mornings when my head was clearer. But life doesn't always cooperate with plans, and I found myself constantly adjusting.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important because the anxiety about falling behind was worse than actually doing the work. Peer discussions also pulled me in occasionally—seeing others active made me feel like I needed to show up too.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt almost automatic, like checking my phone. But then I had a really rough patch emotionally, missed a few days, and that routine just crumbled. I never quite got it back to that consistent place again.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded materials were everything to me—I'm an auditory learner, so being able to listen repeatedly really helped concepts sink in. The discussion forums were also useful because hearing other students' perspectives helped me understand things I'd missed. I'd often replay sections of videos multiple times until something clicked.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and PDFs. I know that sounds bad, but reading when you're anxious is nearly impossible for me—the words just swim on the page. I'd download them with good intentions but rarely actually read them. Some of the optional quizzes I also skipped because they felt like extra pressure I couldn't handle.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually feel myself learning something, like when a concept finally made sense. If it was something I could listen to rather than read, that helped a lot. Also, if it contributed directly to my assignments, I was more motivated. Anything that felt like busywork just added to my anxiety without giving me anything back.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. On one hand, there was no pressure, but on the other hand, I'd stress about whether skipping them would hurt me later. Required activities at least had clear expectations—I knew I had to do them. Optional stuff made me anxious about making the wrong choice, so I often just avoided them entirely.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I was out walking or trying to calm down. I also had a notebook where I'd write things out while listening—that never showed up anywhere online. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos explaining the same concepts in different ways that helped me more than the course materials.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes and listening to downloaded lectures, but I never logged into the LMS once. My best studying often happened offline because being online made me anxious about seeing what I hadn't done yet. The LMS would show nothing, but I was actually working hard during those times.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture, watch maybe five minutes online to make sure it downloaded correctly, and then watch the whole thing later on my laptop offline. The LMS probably showed I barely watched anything, but I'd listened to some lectures three or four times through. It just didn't track any of that.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, and I'd avoid the LMS entirely because just seeing my incomplete tasks made me feel worse. During those times, I was dealing with a lot of sadness and self-doubt. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but breaking them was still really hard.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had multiple assignments due across different courses, I'd shut down completely sometimes. The fatigue from constant anxiety made everything harder—I'd be exhausted even though I hadn't done much. Confusion about what was expected also made me avoid things rather than ask for help, which I know didn't help my situation.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about failing, about the future, about everything. I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though I was technically 'engaged.'
Q15: I postponed exam preparation the most, even though I knew it was crucial. The thought of facing how much I didn't know triggered so much anxiety that avoidance felt safer in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same dread. It's a cycle I'm still learning to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my anxiety and force me to act. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before something was due. It wasn't healthy—I'd be up late, stressed out of my mind—but it was the only way I could push through. Without deadlines, I'd probably never submit anything.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a temporary boost—like maybe I could actually do this. But negative feedback or lower grades would send me spiraling for days. I'd avoid checking my grades sometimes because the anxiety of not knowing felt better than potentially seeing something bad. It's complicated.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but was actually hard for me. Too much freedom meant I had to make a lot of decisions about when and what to study, and that's exhausting when you're anxious. I think I would have done better with more rigid deadlines spread throughout, forcing me to stay on track.
Q19: Having twelve online courses at once made everything harder—there was just too much to keep track of. The discussion forums were good because they made me feel less alone in struggling. But the exam being worth so much of the grade was terrifying and probably contributed to my poor performance there despite doing okay on assignments.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me—logging in frequently out of anxiety, checking things obsessively, but being too overwhelmed to actually complete and submit work. High activity might mean someone is trying really hard but hitting walls they can't get past. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not putting in effort.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some of my best learning happened offline. If someone downloads everything early and studies from their notes, their LMS activity would look minimal even though they're engaged. I wish my exam performance reflected my offline efforts better, but test anxiety really got to me.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in and how often—but they can't show the quality of that time or what's happening emotionally. They miss all the offline work, the mental effort, the struggle behind the screen. They see the what but not the why or the how hard someone is actually trying.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're mentally present, asking questions even if just in your head, and trying to connect new information to what you already know. For me, engagement feels like those rare moments when the anxiety quiets down and I can actually think clearly.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I fought just to show up some days. It can't capture the tears before logging in, or the relief when I finally understood something after struggling for hours offline. It doesn't know about my CBT sessions or how much energy it takes to manage my anxiety while trying to be a student. The numbers don't tell my whole story.
